parts.text:
I'm building an event ticketing system called EventHub using a PEAN stack (PostgreSQL, Express.js, Angular, Node.js).
For now, focus only on the backend (Express.js + PostgreSQL).
I want to follow a strict layered architecture with three layers:
Routes (Presentation) — handles HTTP requests and responses only
Services (Business Logic) — contains all business rules and orchestration
Repositories (Data Access) — all database communication happens here
The dependency rule must be enforced:
Routes may depend on Services
Services may depend on Repositories
No layer may depend on a layer above it
No layer may skip a layer (e.g., Routes must NOT access Repositories directly)
Scaffold the project folder structure and set up the Express app entry point. Do not implement any features yet — only the skeleton with placeholder files that demonstrate where each layer lives.
Include a brief README that explains the dependency rule for future contributors.

toolCalls.arguments:
{"filePath": "/home/jankiz/Developer/Oktatás/szoftver_arch/03/README.md", "content": "# EventHub Backend (PEAN Stack)

## Layered Architecture

This project uses a strict three-layer architecture:

- **Routes (Presentation):** Handles HTTP requests and responses only. No business logic or database access.
- **Services (Business Logic):** Contains all business rules and orchestration. No direct HTTP or database access.
- **Repositories (Data Access):** All database communication happens here. No business logic or HTTP handling.

## Dependency Rule

- Routes may depend on Services
- Services may depend on Repositories
- **No layer may depend on a layer above it**
- **No layer may skip a layer** (e.g., Routes must NOT access Repositories directly)

This rule is enforced to ensure maintainability, testability, and separation of concerns.

## Getting Started

- Entry point: `app.js`
- All features should be implemented following the layer structure above.
"}

```markdown
<!-- Use this file to provide workspace-specific custom instructions to Copilot. For more details, visit https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/copilot-customization#_use-a-githubcopilotinstructionsmd-file -->
- [ ] Verify that the copilot-instructions.md file in the .github directory is created.

- [ ] Clarify Project Requirements
	<!-- Ask for project type, language, and frameworks if not specified. Skip if already provided. -->

- [ ] Scaffold the Project
	<!--
	Ensure that the previous step has been marked as completed.
	Call project setup tool with projectType parameter.
	Run scaffolding command to create project files and folders.
	Use '.' as the working directory.
	If no appropriate projectType is available, search documentation using available tools.
	Otherwise, create the project structure manually using available file creation tools.
	-->

- [ ] Customize the Project
	<!--
	Verify that all previous steps have been completed successfully and you have marked the step as completed.
	Develop a plan to modify codebase according to user requirements.
	Apply modifications using appropriate tools and user-provided references.
	Skip this step for "Hello World" projects.
	-->

- [ ] Install Required Extensions
	<!-- ONLY install extensions provided mentioned in the get_project_setup_info. Skip this step otherwise and mark as completed. -->

- [ ] Compile the Project
	<!--
	Verify that all previous steps have been completed.
	Install any missing dependencies.
	Run diagnostics and resolve any issues.
	Check for markdown files in project folder for relevant instructions on how to do this.
	-->

- [ ] Create and Run Task
	<!--
	Verify that all previous steps have been completed.
	Check https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/debugtest/tasks to determine if the project needs a task. If so, use the create_and_run_task to create and launch a task based on package.json, README.md, and project structure.
	Skip this step otherwise.
	 -->

- [ ] Launch the Project
	<!--
	Verify that all previous steps have been completed.
	Prompt user for debug mode, launch only if confirmed.
	 -->

- [ ] Ensure Documentation is Complete
	<!--
	Verify that all previous steps have been completed.
	Verify that README.md and the copilot-instructions.md file in the .github directory exists and contains current project information.
	Clean up the copilot-instructions.md file in the .github directory by removing all HTML comments.
	 -->

<!--
## Execution Guidelines
PROGRESS TRACKING:
- If any tools are available to manage the above todo list, use it to track progress through this checklist.
- After completing each step, mark it complete and add a summary.
- Read current todo list status before starting each new step.

COMMUNICATION RULES:
- Avoid verbose explanations or printing full command outputs.
- If a step is skipped, state that briefly (e.g. "No extensions needed").
- Do not explain project structure unless asked.
- Keep explanations concise and focused.

DEVELOPMENT RULES:
- Use '.' as the working directory unless user specifies otherwise.
- Avoid adding media or external links unless explicitly requested.
- Use placeholders only with a note that they should be replaced.
- Use VS Code API tool only for VS Code extension projects.
- Once the project is created, it is already opened in Visual Studio Code—do not suggest commands to open this project in Visual Studio again.
- If the project setup information has additional rules, follow them strictly.

FOLDER CREATION RULES:
- Always use the current directory as the project root.
- If you are running any terminal commands, use the '.' argument to ensure that the current working directory is used ALWAYS.
- Do not create a new folder unless the user explicitly requests it besides a .vscode folder for a tasks.json file.
- If any of the scaffolding commands mention that the folder name is not correct, let the user know to create a new folder with the correct name and then reopen it again in vscode.

EXTENSION INSTALLATION RULES:
- Only install extension specified by the get_project_setup_info tool. DO NOT INSTALL any other extensions.

PROJECT CONTENT RULES:
- If the user has not specified project details, assume they want a "Hello World" project as a starting point.
- Avoid adding links of any type (URLs, files, folders, etc.) or integrations that are not explicitly required.
- Avoid generating images, videos, or any other media files unless explicitly requested.
- If you need to use any media assets as placeholders, let the user know that these are placeholders and should be replaced with the actual assets later.
- Ensure all generated components serve a clear purpose within the user's requested workflow.
- If a feature is assumed but not confirmed, prompt the user for clarification before including it.
- If you are working on a VS Code extension, use the VS Code API tool with a query to find relevant VS Code API references and samples related to that query.

TASK COMPLETION RULES:
- Your task is complete when:
  - Project is successfully scaffolded and compiled without errors
  - copilot-instructions.md file in the .github directory exists in the project
  - README.md file exists and is up to date
  - User is provided with clear instructions to debug/launch the project

Before starting a new task in the above plan, update progress in the plan.
-->
- Work through each checklist item systematically.
- Keep communication concise and focused.
- Follow development best practices.
```

parts.text:
Now implement the first feature: listing all events.
Walk through all three layers, top to bottom:

Route: GET /api/events — accepts the request, calls the service, returns the response
Service: retrieves events from the repository. For now, no special business logic — just pass through.
Repository: queries the database and returns the results

Follow the dependency rule strictly. The route must not know anything about the database. The repository must not know anything about HTTP.
Also set up the database connection as a shared infrastructure concern, outside of any layer.

parts.text:
Now implement the ticket purchasing feature. This is where the layered architecture earns its value, because this feature has real business logic.
The business rules are:

A seat can only be sold once (prevent double-booking)
The seat must be locked atomically before payment is attempted
If payment fails, the seat lock must be released (compensation)
The service orchestrates the full flow: check availability → lock seat → process payment → confirm purchase

Implement this across all three layers:

Route: POST /api/tickets/purchase — accepts eventId, seatId, userId. Calls the service. Returns appropriate status codes.
Service: implements the business logic above. Uses the seat repository for data access and a payment service for payment processing. Throws meaningful domain errors (not HTTP errors).
Repository: provides atomic seat locking (the database must guarantee that only one caller can lock a given seat)

For the payment service, create a mock/stub that simulates success and failure — we'll integrate a real gateway later.
Ensure the service layer has zero knowledge of HTTP concepts, and the route layer has zero knowledge of database operations.

edits.text: // Repositories Layer: Data Access for Seats
* Atomically lock a seat for purchase. Returns true if locked, false if already taken.
// Try to lock the seat if not already sold
`UPDATE seats SET locked = TRUE WHERE event_id = $1 AND seat_id = $2 AND locked = FALSE AND sold = FALSE RETURNING *`,
* Release a seat lock (compensation for failed payment)
`UPDATE seats SET locked = FALSE WHERE event_id = $1 AND seat_id = $2 AND sold = FALSE`,
* Mark seat as sold (after successful payment)
`UPDATE seats SET sold = TRUE, locked = FALSE, user_id = $3 WHERE event_id = $1 AND seat_id = $2`,

parts.text:
Our layered architecture needs a consistent error handling strategy that respects layer boundaries.
The principle:

Repositories throw or propagate database-level errors
Services throw domain-specific errors (e.g., NotFoundError, ConflictError, PaymentFailedError) — these describe WHAT went wrong in business terms
Routes do NOT catch errors individually — instead, a centralized error handling middleware translates domain errors into HTTP status codes

Implement:

A set of custom domain error classes
Update the existing services to throw these domain errors
A centralized Express error handling middleware that maps each domain error to the correct HTTP status code and response format

The goal: services never think in HTTP, routes never interpret business meaning from errors. The error handler middleware is the single translation point between the domain world and the HTTP world.

message.text:
Our layered architecture needs a consistent error handling strategy that respects layer boundaries.
The principle:

Repositories throw or propagate database-level errors
Services throw domain-specific errors (e.g., NotFoundError, ConflictError, PaymentFailedError) — these describe WHAT went wrong in business terms
Routes do NOT catch errors individually — instead, a centralized error handling middleware translates domain errors into HTTP status codes

Implement:

A set of custom domain error classes
Update the existing services to throw these domain errors
A centralized Express error handling middleware that maps each domain error to the correct HTTP status code and response format

The goal: services never think in HTTP, routes never interpret business meaning from errors. The error handler middleware is the single translation point between the domain world and the HTTP world.

response.generatedTitle: Created errors.js and errorHandler.js, updated ticketsService.js
Created errors.js and errorHandler.js, updated ticketsService.js

response.value:
A consistent error handling strategy is now implemented:

- Domain error classes (NotFoundError, ConflictError, PaymentFailedError) are defined in src/services/errors.js.
- The service layer throws only domain errors, never HTTP errors.
- Routes do not interpret errors; they pass all errors to next().
- Centralized error handling middleware in

maps domain errors to HTTP status codes and formats responses.
- The Express app uses this middleware as the last handler.

This ensures clean separation: services think in business terms, routes in HTTP, and only the error handler bridges the two. Let me know if you want to extend error handling or add more features.

A centralized Express error handling middleware that maps each domain error to the correct HTTP status code and response format

The goal: services never think in HTTP, routes never interpret business meaning from errors. The error handler middleware is the single translation point between the domain world and the HTTP world. (See <attachments> above for file contents. You may not need to search or read the file again.)

toolCallRounds.response:
A consistent error handling strategy is now implemented:

- Domain error classes (NotFoundError, ConflictError, PaymentFailedError) are defined in src/services/errors.js.
- The service layer throws only domain errors, never HTTP errors.
- Routes do not interpret errors; they pass all errors to next().
- Centralized error handling middleware in src/middleware/errorHandler.js maps domain errors to HTTP status codes and formats responses.
- The Express app uses this middleware as the last handler.

This ensures clean separation: services think in business terms, routes in HTTP, and only the error handler bridges the two. Let me know if you want to extend error handling or add more features.

parts.text:
I want to enforce the layered architecture rules automatically so that no developer (and no AI-generated code) can accidentally violate the dependency rule.
The rules to enforce:
routes/ may only import from services/
services/ may only import from repositories/ and other services
repositories/ may not import from services/ or routes/
No layer may skip a layer
Suggest and set up a tool that analyzes the import/require statements in the codebase and fails if any of these rules are violated. This should be runnable as a script (e.g., npm run arch-test) and integrable into CI.
After setting it up, demonstrate it works by:
Running it against the current (correct) codebase — it should pass
Adding a deliberate violation (e.g., a route importing a repository) — it should fail
Removing the violation — it should pass again

parts.text:
Let's verify that our layered architecture is truly independent between layers.
Create a simple test scenario that proves the following:

The service layer can be tested WITHOUT an HTTP server running (no Express, no supertest — just call the service function directly with a mocked repository)
The repository layer can be tested WITHOUT the service layer (directly call repository functions against a test database)
The route layer can be tested WITHOUT a real database (mock the service, only verify HTTP behavior)

This proves that each layer is independently testable, which is a direct consequence of respecting the dependency rule.
Write one test per layer for the ticket purchasing feature to demonstrate this.

message.text:
Let's verify that our layered architecture is truly independent between layers.

The route layer can be tested WITHOUT a real database (mock the service, only verify HTTP behavior)

This proves that each layer is independently testable, which is a direct consequence of respecting the dependency rule.
Write one test per layer for the ticket purchasing feature to demonstrate this.

- Service layer: test/ticketsService.test.js — tests purchaseTicket with mocked repository and payment, no Express or HTTP.
